summary: My father was born in Chicago, and that's where my grandparents met back in the 1880s. Chicago was a hot spot for French-Canadian immigrants like them, and they raised their family there before retiring back to Canada. So, Chicago played a big role in my family's early history.

Bell Oil and Gas was the company my father worked for when he moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma. He ran tank cars for them after they purchased the business in Arkansas City, Kansas. Bell Oil and Gas was part of my father's career and our family's move to Tulsa.

Tulsa is where I grew up and went to school, including Barnard, Wilson, and Central High School. My father moved here for work with Bell Oil and Gas, and I eventually started my business career here. Tulsa has been home for most of my life and the place where I built my family and career.

My mother came from Texas and worked as a secretary before marrying my father. She was a huge influence on my life, especially in terms of faith and values. Texas is part of my family's background through my mother.

My father got a job opportunity in Arkansas City, Kansas, running tank cars before Bell Oil and Gas purchased the business. That job was a stepping stone that eventually led our family to Tulsa. Arkansas City was an important place in my father's early career.

My grandparents were French-Canadian and eventually retired back to a small town in Canada after living in Chicago. They even ran a grocery store there. Canada is part of my heritage and family roots.

I attended Barnard Elementary School here in Tulsa as part of my early education. It was one of the first schools I went to before moving on to Wilson Elementary and Central High. Barnard was where my schooling began in Tulsa.
After Barnard, I went to Wilson Elementary School in Tulsa. It was part of my formative years before I attended Central High School. Wilson helped shape my early education experience.

I graduated from Central High School in Tulsa with the class of 1950. It was a significant time in my life before I went on to the University of Oklahoma. Central High was where I took some of the hardest math classes and began to figure out my future path.

I attended the University of Oklahoma after high school, initially planning to be an engineer. I joined ROTC there and became a 2nd Lieutenant, but after the Korean War ended, I switched from flight school to becoming an intercept controller. OU was where I shifted my career path and gained valuable education.

The Korean War had just ended when I was in flight school, which meant there were too many pilots. Because of that, I was offered the chance to leave flight school and take on a different role as an intercept controller. The war's timing influenced my military career significantly.

I worked with the Strategic Air Command in Bartlesville, Oklahoma, where I was an intercept controller. We practiced tracking and scrambling fighters to keep the Russians away during the Cold War era. SAC was a key part of my military service and involved intense radar and jamming exercises.

I was stationed in Bartlesville, Oklahoma, during my time with the Strategic Air Command. It was an unusual location for such a role, but we conducted nightly exercises to track and intercept potential threats. Bartlesville was where I gained important military experience.

After my military service, I worked as a salesman for Manica Kanzee Printing Company, which specialized in business forms and accounting technology. I was an outside salesman covering Tulsa and surrounding areas. This job was my first real business experience after the Air Force.

I originally planned to attend Oklahoma A&M College to study engineering, inspired by a neighbor who was a Navy pilot and Sigma Nu there. However, I realized engineering wasn't for me and switched to the University of Oklahoma. Oklahoma A&M later became Oklahoma State University.

Oklahoma A&M College, where I initially planned to study, eventually became Oklahoma State University. Although I didn't attend OSU, the transition made sense in hindsight as I chose to study business at the University of Oklahoma instead. OSU represents part of the educational landscape I considered.

My father built a tank car business in St. Louis with a friend, running tank cars out of there while still working for Bell Oil & Gas. This entrepreneurial spirit influenced me greatly. St. Louis was part of my father's business ventures outside of Tulsa.

I was one of the original investors and co-founders of QuikTrip, starting with a $5,000 investment loaned by my father. Burt Holmes started QuikTrip and brought me in because I wanted a simple, low-cost business with little competition. We began with a small investment and learned the convenience store business from the ground up.

Burt Holmes was the man who started QuikTrip and invited me to join as an investor and partner. He had experience in insurance and loved starting businesses, even at nearly 78 years old. Burt's vision and leadership were crucial in launching QuikTrip and shaping my business career.

Burt Holmes, who started QuikTrip, was involved in the insurance business through Holmes Group Insurance. He worked for his dad and later ran his own insurance companies, selling Holmes Group Insurance a few years ago. This background gave Burt a strong business foundation before starting QuikTrip.

Burt Holmes had observed the 7-Eleven stores in Dallas, which influenced the concept behind QuikTrip. I also knew about 7-Eleven because my mother had family down there. Dallas was part of the inspiration and context for starting a convenience store business in Tulsa.

I was aware of 7-Eleven convenience stores, especially because my mother had family in Texas near Dallas where 7-Eleven was popular. Burt Holmes had also watched 7-Eleven stores in Dallas, which helped shape our approach to QuikTrip. 7-Eleven served as a model for the convenience store business we wanted to build.

Git-n-Go was another convenience store chain that started later, after we had already begun QuikTrip. While I didn't have a direct role with Git-n-Go, it was part of the evolving convenience store market in Tulsa and the region. It represents the competition and growth in the industry we helped pioneer.
